A system is built on the assumption that progress can be made by generating candidates in language, criticizing them in language, and recombining material drawn from a background pool, a universal pool, and a novel pool. In ordinary cases, this may look productive. But the premises identify two hard limits.

First, some target novelties are not new explanations at all. They require inventing a measurement or an experimental protocol. In such a domain, verbal search can at best describe the need for a measurement; it does not itself supply the instrument, operation, or discriminating procedure. So the architecture can continue producing articulate candidates while remaining unable to cross the real frontier.

Second, even when the target is theoretical, language may be too weak a medium for the search. Einstein’s route from an intuition to field equations required ten years of mathematics. If that path is not approximable by verbal exploration, then a language-centered architecture can circle around the insight without reaching the formal structure that makes it true. It may generate elegant paraphrases of a gap it cannot close.

These limits interact with the pool-mixing problem. If background, universal, and novel pools are mixed incorrectly, the system launders familiar fragments into explanations that look new. Because the search is mostly linguistic, the laundering can be hard to detect: novelty of phrasing can masquerade as novelty of method or theory. In edge cases requiring a new protocol or nonverbal mathematical development, this means the system can appear inventive precisely when it is most trapped in the basin of known ideas.

Now add the social condition: widespread rapid compromises turn steals into a nuisance. If appropriation is quickly settled everywhere, then taking fragments from existing sources ceases to be a major event. In that world, laundering familiar material becomes even easier to tolerate. Reused pieces are not treated as profound thefts, only as annoyances to be smoothed over. The architecture’s failure mode therefore becomes socially stabilized: familiar fragments can circulate rapidly under minor dispute while being accepted as apparent progress.

The crows fit this world exactly as a local test case. The vengeful crows presume the photos captured their ankle. Their grievance is keyed not to a whole crow, but to a particular identifying feature. A photo, then, need not represent the total subject to trigger recognition of capture; a partial trace is enough if it latches onto the right marker. By analogy, the architecture may seize or be accused of seizing only “the ankle” of an idea: a fragment distinctive enough to count as capture, even if embedded in new wording. Since compromises are rapid, such captures are downgraded to nuisances.

So the resulting world is one where a language-first system often cannot produce the deepest novelties, yet can still manufacture the appearance of novelty by rearranging recognizable parts. Real breakthroughs that require new measurements or extended mathematics remain out of reach, while partial captures of old ideas proliferate and are socially normalized.
